mn43:0.2: The Greater Series of Questions and Answers
mn43:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn43:1.3: Then, in the evening, the Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita, having risen from retreat, approached the Venerable Sāriputta. Having approached, he exchanged greetings with the Venerable Sāriputta.
mn43:1.4: When they had concluded their courteous and memorable conversation, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita said this to the Venerable Sāriputta:
mn43:10.1: “With mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇa*] purified and freed from the five *indriyas*, friend, what can one know?”
mn43:10.2: “With mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇa*] purified and freed from the five *indriyas*, friend, one can know the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*] as ‘space is infinite’; one can know the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*] as ‘consciousness is infinite’; one can know the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*] as ‘there is nothing.’”
mn43:11.1: “But friend, by what does one understand a knowable *dhamma*?”
mn43:11.2: “Friend, one understands a knowable *dhamma* with the eye of discernment [*paññā*].”
mn43:12.1: “But friend, for what purpose is discernment [*paññā*]?”
mn43:12.2: “Friend, discernment [*paññā*] is for direct knowledge, for full understanding, for abandoning.”
mn43:13.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the arising of right view?”
mn43:13.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for the arising of right view—
mn43:13.3: the voice of another and appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikāra*].
mn43:13.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the arising of right view.”
mn43:14.1: “And friend, supported by how many factors does right view become fruitful in liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*], and become fruitful in liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*]?”
mn43:14.2: “Supported by five factors, friend, right view becomes fruitful in liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*], and becomes fruitful in liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*].
mn43:14.3: Here, friend, right view is supported by virtue, supported by learning, supported by discussion, supported by settling [*samathānuggahitā*], supported by insight [*vipassanānuggahitā*].
mn43:14.4: Supported by these five factors, friend, right view becomes fruitful in liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*], and becomes fruitful in liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] and fruitful in the benefits of liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*].”
mn43:15.1: “How many kinds of *bhava* are there, friend?”
mn43:16.1: “And how, friend, does renewed rebirth in the future come to be?”
mn43:16.2: “For beings with blindness [*avijjā*] as hindrance and *taṇhā* as fetter, there is delighting now here, now there—
mn43:16.3: thus renewed rebirth in the future comes to be.”
mn43:17.1: “And how, friend, does renewed rebirth in the future not come to be?”
mn43:17.2: “With the fading away of blindness [*avijjā*], with the arising of knowledge, with the cessation of *taṇhā*—
mn43:17.3: thus renewed rebirth in the future does not come to be.”
mn43:18.2: “Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, with thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*]—
mn43:18.3: this is called the first *jhāna*, friend.”
mn43:19.1: “And the first *jhāna*, friend, has how many factors?”
mn43:19.3: Here, friend, for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the first *jhāna*, thought [*vitakka*] continues, examination [*vicāra*] continues, *pīti* continues, *sukha* continues, and singleness of mind [*cittekaggatā*] continues.
mn43:19.4: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors.”
mn43:2.1: “‘One of poor discernment [*duppañño*], one of poor discernment [*duppañño*],’ friend, they say.
mn43:2.2: In what way, friend, is one called of poor discernment [*duppañño*]?”
mn43:2.3: “‘He does not understand, he does not understand,’ friend; that is why one is called of poor discernment [*duppañño*].
mn43:2.4: And what does he not understand?
mn43:2.5: He does not understand, ‘This is *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn43:2.6: ‘He does not understand, he does not understand,’ friend; that is why one is called of poor discernment [*duppañño*].”
mn43:2.7: “*Sādhu*, friend,” the Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita said, delighting in and approving the Venerable Sāriputta’s words. Then he asked the Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn43:20.1: “And the first *jhāna*, friend, has what factors abandoned and what factors present?”
mn43:20.2: “The first *jhāna*, friend, has five factors abandoned and five factors present.
mn43:20.3: Here, friend, for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the first *jhāna*, sensual desire is abandoned, ill will is abandoned, sloth and drowsiness are abandoned, restlessness and remorse are abandoned, and doubt is abandoned;
mn43:20.4: thought [*vitakka*] continues, examination [*vicāra*] continues, *pīti* continues, *sukha* continues, and singleness of mind [*cittekaggatā*] continues.
mn43:20.5: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors abandoned and five factors present.”
mn43:21.1: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different fields and different domains, and they do not experience one another’s field and domain. That is to say—
mn43:21.2: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:21.3: For these five *indriyas*, friend, with different fields and different domains, not experiencing one another’s field and domain, what is their support, and who experiences their field and domain?”
mn43:21.4: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different fields and different domains, and they do not experience one another’s field and domain. That is to say—
mn43:21.5: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:21.6: For these five *indriyas*, friend, with different fields and different domains, not experiencing one another’s field and domain, mind is their support, and mind experiences their field and domain.”
mn43:22.11: “Just now, friend, we understand the Venerable Sāriputta’s statement thus:
mn43:22.13: But just now, friend, we also understand the Venerable Sāriputta’s statement thus:
mn43:22.15: How should the meaning of this statement be seen?”
mn43:22.16: “Then, friend, I shall give you a simile; for some wise people here understand the meaning of a statement by means of a simile.
mn43:22.18: Just as, friend, when an oil lamp is burning, its radiance is seen depending on the flame, and the flame is seen depending on the radiance,
mn43:22.19: even so, friend, life stands depending on heat, and heat stands depending on life.”
mn43:22.2: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:22.3: Depending on what do these five *indriyas* stand?”
mn43:22.5: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:22.6: These five *indriyas*, friend, stand depending on life.”
mn43:22.7: “And life, friend, stands depending on what?”
mn43:22.9: “And heat, friend, stands depending on what?”
mn43:23.1: “Then, friend, are the life-*saṅkhāras* [*āyusaṅkhārā*] the very same as feelable *dhammas* [*vedaniyā dhammā*], or are the life-*saṅkhāras* [*āyusaṅkhārā*] one thing and the feelable *dhammas* [*vedaniyā dhammā*] another?”
mn43:23.2: “Friend, the life-*saṅkhāras* [*āyusaṅkhārā*] are not the very same as the feelable *dhammas* [*vedaniyā dhammā*].
mn43:23.3: For if, friend, the life-*saṅkhāras* [*āyusaṅkhārā*] were the very same as the feelable *dhammas* [*vedaniyā dhammā*], then for a *bhikkhu* who had attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*], emergence would not be discerned.
mn43:23.4: But because, friend, the life-*saṅkhāras* [*āyusaṅkhārā*] are one thing and the feelable *dhammas* [*vedaniyā dhammā*] another, therefore for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*], emergence is discerned.”
mn43:24.1: “When, friend, how many *dhammas* leave this body, so that this body lies cast aside and flung away, like a senseless log?”
mn43:24.2: “When, friend, three *dhammas* leave this body—life, heat, and consciousness [*viññāṇa*]—then this body lies cast aside and flung away, like a senseless log.”
mn43:25.1: “This one, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, and this *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*]—what is the distinction between them?”
mn43:25.2: “For this one, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, the bodily *saṅkhāras* [*kāyasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, the verbal *saṅkhāras* [*vacīsaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, the mind *saṅkhāras* [*cittasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, life is exhausted, heat is stilled, and the *indriyas* are broken up.
mn43:25.3: And for this *bhikkhu*, friend, who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*], the bodily *saṅkhāras* [*kāyasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, the verbal *saṅkhāras* [*vacīsaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, the mind *saṅkhāras* [*cittasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and been stilled, but life is not exhausted, heat is not stilled, and the *indriyas* are clear.
mn43:25.4: This one, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, and this *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*]—this is their distinction.”
mn43:26.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the attainment of the neither-painful-nor-pleasant liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*]?”
mn43:26.2: “There are four conditions, friend, for the attainment of the neither-painful-nor-pleasant liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*].
mn43:26.3: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and sorrow, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-painful-nor-pleasant, with purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn43:26.4: These, friend, are the four conditions for the attainment of the neither-painful-nor-pleasant liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*].”
mn43:27.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the attainment of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:27.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for the attainment of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:27.3: non-attention to all *nimittas*, and attention to the *animitta* element [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:27.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the attainment of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:27.5: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the stability of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:28.1: “There are three conditions, friend, for the stability of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:28.2: non-attention to all *nimittas*, attention to the *animitta* element [*animittāya dhātuyā*], and prior determination.
mn43:28.3: These, friend, are the three conditions for the stability of the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for emerging from the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:29.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for emerging from the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:29.3: attention to all *nimittas*, and non-attention to the *animitta* element [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:29.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for emerging from the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.5: “And this immeasurable liberation of mind [*appamāṇā cetovimutti*], and the liberation of mind of nothingness [*ākiñcaññā cetovimutti*], and the empty liberation of mind [*suññatā cetovimutti*], and the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittā cetovimutti*]—are these *dhammas* different in meaning as well as different in expression, or are they one in meaning, differing only in expression?”
mn43:3.1: “‘One with discernment [*paññavā*], one with discernment [*paññavā*],’ friend, they say.
mn43:3.2: In what way, friend, is one called one with discernment [*paññavā*]?”
mn43:3.3: “‘He understands, he understands,’ friend; that is why one is called one with discernment [*paññavā*].
mn43:3.5: He understands, ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn43:3.6: ‘He understands, he understands,’ friend; that is why one is called one with discernment [*paññavā*].”
mn43:30.1: “Friend, there is a way in which, regarding this immeasurable liberation of mind [*appamāṇā cetovimutti*], the liberation of mind of nothingness [*ākiñcaññā cetovimutti*], the empty liberation of mind [*suññatā cetovimutti*], and the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittā cetovimutti*], these *dhammas* are different in meaning as well as different in expression;
mn43:30.2: and there is also a way in which, regarding them, these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in expression.
mn43:31.1: And what, friend, is the way in which these *dhammas* are different in meaning as well as different in expression?
mn43:31.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu* dwells pervading one direction with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.
mn43:31.3: With a mind accompanied by compassion [*karuṇā*] …
mn43:31.4: with a mind accompanied by gladness [*muditā*] …
mn43:31.5: with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*] he dwells pervading one direction, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.
mn43:31.6: This is called the immeasurable liberation of mind [*appamāṇā cetovimutti*], friend.
mn43:32.1: And what, friend, is the liberation of mind of nothingness [*ākiñcaññā cetovimutti*]?
mn43:32.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], aware that ‘there is nothing.’
mn43:32.3: This is called the liberation of mind of nothingness [*ākiñcaññā cetovimutti*], friend.
mn43:33.1: And what, friend, is the empty liberation of mind [*suññatā cetovimutti*]?
mn43:33.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
mn43:33.3: ‘This is empty of self or of what belongs to self.’
mn43:33.4: This is called the empty liberation of mind [*suññatā cetovimutti*], friend.
mn43:34.1: And what, friend, is the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittā cetovimutti*]?
mn43:34.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, through non-attention to all *nimittas*, enters and dwells in the *animitta* *samādhi* of mind [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn43:34.3: This is called the *animitta* liberation of mind [*animittā cetovimutti*], friend.
mn43:34.4: This, friend, is the way in which these *dhammas* are different in meaning as well as different in expression.
mn43:35.1: And what, friend, is the way in which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in expression?
mn43:35.2: Greed, friend, is a source of limitation; hatred is a source of limitation; delusion is a source of limitation.
mn43:35.3: For a *bhikkhu* whose *āsavas* are destroyed, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:35.4: Of whatever immeasurable liberations of mind [*appamāṇā cetovimuttiyo*] there are, the unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is declared the highest among them.
mn43:35.5: And that unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is empty of greed, empty of hatred, empty of delusion.
mn43:36.1: Greed, friend, is something; hatred is something; delusion is something.
mn43:36.2: For a *bhikkhu* whose *āsavas* are destroyed, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:36.3: Of whatever liberations of mind of nothingness [*ākiñcaññā cetovimuttiyo*] there are, the unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is declared the highest among them.
mn43:36.4: And that unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is empty of greed, empty of hatred, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.1: Greed, friend, is a maker of *nimitta*; hatred is a maker of *nimitta*; delusion is a maker of *nimitta*.
mn43:37.2: For a *bhikkhu* whose *āsavas* are destroyed, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:37.3: Of whatever *animitta* liberations of mind [*animittā cetovimuttiyo*] there are, the unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is declared the highest among them.
mn43:37.4: And that unshakable liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] is empty of greed, empty of hatred, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.5: This, friend, is the way in which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in expression.”
mn43:37.6: This is what the Venerable Sāriputta said.
mn43:37.7: Satisfied, the Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita delighted in the Venerable Sāriputta’s words.
mn43:37.8: The Greater Series of Questions and Answers is finished, the third.
mn43:4.2: In what way, friend, is it called consciousness [*viññāṇa*]?”
mn43:4.3: “‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend; that is why it is called consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn43:4.5: It cognizes *sukha*; it cognizes *dukkha*; it cognizes what is neither-painful-nor-pleasant.
mn43:4.6: ‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend; that is why it is called consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn43:5.2: are these *dhammas* bound up together, or are they separate?
mn43:5.3: And is it possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them?”
mn43:5.5: these *dhammas* are bound up together, not separate.
mn43:5.6: And it is not possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them.
mn43:5.7: For what one understands, that one cognizes; and what one cognizes, that one understands.
mn43:5.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are bound up together, not separate.
mn43:5.9: And it is not possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them.”
mn43:6.2: for these *dhammas*, bound up together and not separate, what is the distinction?”
mn43:6.4: of these *dhammas*, bound up together and not separate, discernment [*paññā*] is to be developed [*bhāvetabbā*], consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is to be fully understood.
mn43:7.2: In what way, friend, is it called *vedanā*?”
mn43:7.3: “‘One feels, one feels,’ friend; that is why it is called *vedanā*.
mn43:7.5: One feels *sukha*, one feels *dukkha*, one feels what is neither-painful-nor-pleasant.
mn43:7.6: ‘One feels, one feels,’ friend; that is why it is called *vedanā*.”
mn43:8.2: In what way, friend, is it called *saññā*?”
mn43:8.3: “‘One perceives, one perceives,’ friend; that is why it is called *saññā*.
mn43:8.5: One perceives blue, one perceives yellow, one perceives red, one perceives white.
mn43:8.6: ‘One perceives, one perceives,’ friend; that is why it is called *saññā*.”
mn43:9.2: are these *dhammas* bound up together, or are they separate?
mn43:9.3: And is it possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them?”
mn43:9.5: these *dhammas* are bound up together, not separate.
mn43:9.6: And it is not possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them.
mn43:9.7: For what one feels, that one perceives; what one perceives, that one cognizes.
mn43:9.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are bound up together, not separate.
mn43:9.9: And it is not possible, by disentangling these *dhammas* from one another, to describe the difference between them.”
